title: Qubit-efficient variational algorithm for nuclear structure
abstract: In this work, we compare three qubit-mapping strategies to study the structure of the nuclear ground state within the shell model description employing the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) approach. Although the initial point for different mappings is a Hamiltonian matrix in many-body particle basis or Slater determinant (SD) basis, the structure of the trial wavefunction and resource counts are different for each mapping. These three mappings are tested for a mid $p$-shell nucleus $^{10}$B and compared the quantum resources required to find the ground state for each mapping. Further, we extend the qubit-efficient mapping to study the ground state of one more mid $p$-shell nucleus $^{12}$C. We run circuits up to 26-qubits representing their ground states on a noisy simulator (IBM's FakeFez backend) and quantum hardware ($ibm\_fez$). The best post-error mitigated results from the hardware for $^{10}$B ground state is obtained following SD to qubit mapping with a percent error of 0.21 \%. The percent errors for the same state following cSD and pnSD mapping are 3.37 and 8.88 \%, respectively. On the other hand, following the cSD mapping, the post-error mitigated ground state energy of $^{12}$C is 6.82 \% away from the exact result. We further evaluate the fidelity of the VQE wavefunctions obtained from hardware with respect to the shell model wavefunctions for the cSD mapping. This cSD mapping can be useful for scaling the VQE algorithm for complex nuclei across different mass regions in terms of qubit efficiency.
summary: This paper investigates how different methods (mappings) can encode the complex Hamiltonian of atomic nuclei into a quantum circuit for use with the VQE algorithm. By testing SD, pnSD, and cSD mappings on $^{10}	ext{B}$ and $^{12}	ext{C}$, the authors compare qubit efficiency, hardware performance, and energy accuracy. The findings guide the choice of mapping, suggesting cSD is best for qubit count but SD showed best hardware performance for the tested state.
main_problem: The paper compares different qubit-mapping strategies to study the ground state structure of atomic nuclei using the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) approach.
main_result: The SD mapping yielded the best post-error mitigated result for the $^{10}	ext{B}$ ground state on hardware (0.21% error), while the cSD mapping was shown to be the most qubit-efficient for scaling to complex nuclei.
method: The study employs VQE on a noisy simulator and quantum hardware, comparing three mappings (SD, pnSD, cSD) to encode the nuclear Hamiltonian into qubit circuits.
model_or_system: The physical system is nuclear structure, modeled using the shell model description for mid p-shell nuclei ($^{10}	ext{B}$ and $^{12}	ext{C}$). The Hamiltonian is represented in either a many-body particle basis or a Slater determinant (SD) basis.
key_observables: Ground state energy (percent error relative to exact result), wavefunction fidelity, and quantum resource counts (qubits, circuit depth).
important_parameters: The nuclei studied are $^{10}	ext{B}$ and $^{12}	ext{C}$. The resource comparison highlights the qubit count (SD: 26 qubits vs. cSD: 5 qubits for $^{10}	ext{B}$).
main_assumptions: The comparison assumes the applicability of VQE and the effectiveness of error mitigation techniques (like ZNE) on current quantum hardware.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the single-particle states, qubit assignments for the three mappings, and comparisons of energy errors ($\Delta E$) and wavefunction fidelities ($F$) across different noise regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem by comparing three mappings (SD, pnSD, cSD) for VQE. It details the resource comparison (qubits, depth) and presents results on both noisy simulators and quantum hardware, concluding with an assessment of mapping utility for scaling.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the resource scaling challenge in applying quantum algorithms to complex many-body physics, providing concrete benchmarks for mapping strategies that are crucial for near-term quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The 12 single particle states representing the p-shell is shown. The quantum numbers, n, l, j, m, are given in usual nuclear shell model notation.

Figure 2: (a) 26-qubit circuit for 10B ground state following SD mapping, (b) 20-qubit circuit for the same nucleus considering pnSD mapping, (c) 5-qubit circuit for the ground state of 10B using cSD .
Figure 3: Convergence of 10B ground state binding energies with the number of iterations using (a) Cobyla optimizer and (b) Slsqp optimizer.
Figure 4: ZNE implemented for (a) noisy simulated results and (b) hardware results.
Figure 5: The difference in the ground state binding energies, ∆E, between the error-mitigated results from the noisy simulator and IBM hardware are com- pared to the shell model results.
